From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Pre-assembled firearm ammunition

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Cartridge_cross_section.svg/250px-Cartridge_cross_section.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Cartridge_cross_section.svg)

A modern round consists of the following:

1. *Bullet*, as the [projectile](/wiki/Projectile "Projectile")
2. *Cartridge case*, which holds all parts together
3. *[Propellant](/wiki/Propellant "Propellant")*, for example, [gunpowder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder") or [cordite](/wiki/Cordite "Cordite")
4. *[Rim](/wiki/Rim_(firearms) "Rim (firearms)")*, which allows the firearm's extractor to grip the casing to remove it from the chamber
5. [*Primer*](/wiki/Centerfire_ammunition#Centerfire_primers "Centerfire ammunition"), which ignites the propellant

A **cartridge**,[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) also known as a **round**, is a type of pre-assembled firearm ammunition packaging a [projectile](/wiki/Projectile "Projectile") ([bullet](/wiki/Bullet "Bullet"), [shot](/wiki/Shot_(pellet) "Shot (pellet)"), or [slug](/wiki/Shotgun_slug "Shotgun slug")), a [propellant](/wiki/Propellant "Propellant") substance ([smokeless powder](/wiki/Smokeless_powder "Smokeless powder"), [black powder substitute](/wiki/Black_powder_substitute "Black powder substitute"), or [black powder](/wiki/Black_powder "Black powder")) and an ignition device ([primer](/wiki/Primer_(firearm) "Primer (firearm)") or [percussion cap](/wiki/Percussion_cap "Percussion cap")) within a [metallic](/wiki/Metallic_cartridge "Metallic cartridge"), [paper](/wiki/Paper_cartridge "Paper cartridge"), or [plastic case](/wiki/Polymer-cased_ammunition "Polymer-cased ammunition") that is precisely made to fit within the [barrel](/wiki/Gun_barrel "Gun barrel") [chamber](/wiki/Chamber_(firearms) "Chamber (firearms)") of a [breechloading](/wiki/Breechloading "Breechloading") gun, for convenient transportation and handling during shooting.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Although in popular usage the term "bullet" is often used to refer to a complete cartridge, the correct usage only refers to the projectile.

Military and commercial producers continue to pursue the goal of [caseless ammunition](/wiki/Caseless_ammunition "Caseless ammunition"). Some [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery") ammunition uses the same cartridge concept as found in [small arms](/wiki/Small_arms "Small arms"). In other cases, the [artillery shell](/wiki/Shell_(projectile) "Shell (projectile)") is separate from the propellant charge.

A cartridge without a projectile is called a *[blank](/wiki/Blank_(cartridge) "Blank (cartridge)")*; one that is completely [inert](/wiki/Inert_munitions "Inert munitions") (contains no active primer and no propellant) is called a *[dummy](/wiki/Dummy_round "Dummy round")*; one that failed to ignite and shoot off the projectile is called a *[dud](/wiki/Dud "Dud")*; and one that ignited but failed to sufficiently push the projectile out of the barrel is called a *[squib](/wiki/Squib_load "Squib load")*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Cartridge_Sample_2.jpg/500px-Cartridge_Sample_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cartridge_Sample_2.jpg)

A variety of rifle cartridges: (**1**) [.17 HM2](/wiki/.17_HM2 ".17 HM2"); (**2**) [.17 HMR](/wiki/.17_HMR ".17 HMR"); (**3**) [.22 LR](/wiki/.22_LR ".22 LR"); (**4**) [.22 Win Mag R/F.22 WMR](/wiki/.22_Winchester_Magnum_Rimfire ".22 Winchester Magnum Rimfire"); (**5**) [.17/23 SMc](/w/index.php?title=.17/23_SMc&action=edit&redlink=1 ".17/23 SMc (page does not exist)"); (**6**) [5mm/35 SMc](/wiki/5mm/35_SMc "5mm/35 SMc"); (**7**) [.22 Hornet](/wiki/.22_Hornet ".22 Hornet"); (**8**) [.223 Remington](/wiki/.223_Remington ".223 Remington"); (**9**) [.223 WSSM](/wiki/.223_WSSM ".223 WSSM"); (**10**) [.243 Win](/wiki/.243_Win ".243 Win"); (**11**) [.243 Win Improved (Ackley)](/wiki/P.O._Ackley#Wildcats_and_Ackley_Improved_Cartridges "P.O. Ackley"); (**12**) [.25-06 Remington](/wiki/.25-06_Remington ".25-06 Remington"); (**13**) [.270 Winchester](/wiki/.270_Winchester ".270 Winchester"); (**14**) [.308 Winchester](/wiki/.308_Winchester ".308 Winchester"); (**15**) [.30-06 Springfield](/wiki/.30-06_Springfield ".30-06 Springfield"); (**16**) [.45-70 Government](/wiki/.45-70 ".45-70"); (**17**) [.50-90 Sharps](/wiki/.50-90_Sharps ".50-90 Sharps")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Big_caliber_cartridge_comparison_withDATA.jpg/500px-Big_caliber_cartridge_comparison_withDATA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Big_caliber_cartridge_comparison_withDATA.jpg)

## Type

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cartridge_(firearms)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Type")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Cartridge_(firearms) "Special:EditPage/Cartridge (firearms)") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(November 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Cartridges can be categorized by the type of primer, which is a small charge of an [impact](/wiki/Shock_sensitivity "Shock sensitivity")-sensitive explosive compound located:

* At the center of the [case head](#Case) ([centerfire](/wiki/Centerfire "Centerfire"))
* Inside the [rim](/wiki/Rim_(firearms) "Rim (firearms)") ([rimfire](/wiki/Rimfire_ammunition "Rimfire ammunition"))
* Inside the walls on the fold of the case base that is shaped like a cup (cupfire)
* In a sideways projection that is shaped like a pin ([pinfire](/wiki/Pinfire "Pinfire"))
* In a lip ([lipfire](/wiki/Ethan_Allen_(armsmaker)#Cartridge_pistols "Ethan Allen (armsmaker)"))
* In a small bulge shaped like a nipple at the case base ([teatfire](/wiki/Teat-fire_cartridge "Teat-fire cartridge"))
* By an [electric-sensitive](/wiki/Electronic_firing "Electronic firing") chemical mixture
* By a needle-like firing pin that pierces the case and hits a percussion cap ([needle gun](/wiki/Needle_gun "Needle gun"))

Only small-caliber rimfire cartridges and centerfire cartridges have survived into the modern day.

## Design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cartridge_(firearms)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Design")]

### Purpose

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cartridge_(firearms)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Purpose")]

The cartridge was invented specifically for [breechloading](/wiki/Breechloading "Breechloading") firearms. Prior to its invention, the projectiles and propellant were carried separately and had to be individually loaded [via the muzzle](/wiki/Muzzleloading "Muzzleloading") into the [gun barrel](/wiki/Gun_barrel "Gun barrel") before firing, then have a separate [ignitor](/wiki/Fire_making "Fire making") compound (from a burning [slow match](/wiki/Slow_match "Slow match"), to a small charge of [gunpowder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder") in a [flash pan](/wiki/Flash_pan "Flash pan"), to a metallic [percussion cap](/wiki/Percussion_cap "Percussion cap") mounted on top of a "nipple" or cone), to serve as a source of [activation energy](/wiki/Activation_energy "Activation energy") to set off the shot. Such loading procedures often require adding paper/cloth [wadding](/wiki/Wadding "Wadding") and [ramming down repeatedly with a rod](/wiki/Ramrod "Ramrod") to optimize the [gas seal](/wiki/Seal_(mechanical) "Seal (mechanical)"), and are thus clumsy and inconvenient, severely restricting the practical [rate of fire](/wiki/Rate_of_fire "Rate of fire") of the weapon, leaving the shooter vulnerable to the threat of [close combat](/wiki/Close_combat "Close combat") (particularly [cavalry](/wiki/Cavalry "Cavalry") [charges](/wiki/Charge_(warfare) "Charge (warfare)")) as well as complicating the logistics of ammunition.

The primary purpose of using a cartridge is to offer a handy pre-assembled "all-in-one" package that is convenient to handle and transport, easily loaded into the breech (rear end) of the barrel, as well as preventing potential propellant loss, contamination or degradation from [moisture](/wiki/Moisture "Moisture") and the elements. In modern [self-loading firearms](/wiki/Repeating_firearm#Autoloading "Repeating firearm"), the cartridge case also enables the [action](/wiki/Action_(firearms) "Action (firearms)") mechanism to use part of the [propellant](/wiki/Propellant "Propellant")'s energy (carried inside the cartridge itself) and cyclically load new rounds of ammunition to allow quick repeated firing.

To perform a firing, the round is first inserted into a "ready" position within the [chamber](/wiki/Chamber_(firearms) "Chamber (firearms)") aligned with the [bore axis](/wiki/Bore_axis "Bore axis") (i.e. "in *battery*"). While in the chamber, the cartridge case [obturates](/wiki/Obturation "Obturation") all other directions except the [bore](/wiki/Gun_barrel#Bore "Gun barrel") to the front, reinforced by a [breechblock](/wiki/Breechblock "Breechblock") or a locked [bolt](/wiki/Bolt_(firearms) "Bolt (firearms)") from behind, designating the forward direction as the [path of least resistance](/wiki/Path_of_least_resistance "Path of least resistance"). When the [trigger](/wiki/Trigger_(firearms) "Trigger (firearms)") is pulled, the [sear](/wiki/Sear_(firearm) "Sear (firearm)") disengages and releases the [hammer](/wiki/Hammer_(firearms) "Hammer (firearms)")/[striker](/wiki/Firing_pin "Firing pin"), causing the [firing pin](/wiki/Firing_pin "Firing pin") to impact the [primer](/wiki/Primer_(firearms) "Primer (firearms)") embedded in the base of the cartridge. The [shock-sensitive](/wiki/Shock_sensitivity "Shock sensitivity") chemical in the primer then creates a [jet](/wiki/Jet_(fluid) "Jet (fluid)") of [sparks](/wiki/Spark_(fire) "Spark (fire)") that travels into the case and ignites the main propellant charge within, causing the powders to *[deflagrate](/wiki/Deflagration "Deflagration")* (but not *[detonate](/wiki/Detonation "Detonation")*). This rapid [exothermic](/wiki/Exothermic "Exothermic") [combustion](/wiki/Combustion "Combustion") [yields](/wiki/Yield_(chemistry) "Yield (chemistry)") a mixture of highly energetic gases and generates a very high [pressure](/wiki/Pressure "Pressure") inside the case, often [fire-forming](/wiki/Fire_forming "Fire forming") it against the chamber wall. When the pressure builds up sufficiently to overcome the fastening [friction](/wiki/Friction "Friction") between the projectile (e.g. bullet) and the case neck, the projectile will detach from the case and, pushed by the expanding high-pressure gases behind it, [move down the bore](/wiki/Internal_ballistics "Internal ballistics") and out the [muzzle](/wiki/Muzzle_(firearms) "Muzzle (firearms)") at [extremely high speed](/wiki/Muzzle_velocity "Muzzle velocity"). After the bullet exits the barrel, the gases are released to the surroundings as [ejectae](/wiki/Ejecta "Ejecta") in a [loud blast](/wiki/Muzzle_blast "Muzzle blast"), and the chamber pressure drops back down to [ambient level](/wiki/Ambient_pressure "Ambient pressure"). The case, which had been elastically expanded by high pressure, contracts slightly, which eases its removal from the chamber when pulled by the [extractor](/wiki/Extractor_(firearms) "Extractor (firearms)"). The spent cartridge, with its projectile and propellant gone but the case still containing a used-up primer, then gets ejected from the gun to clear room for a subsequent new round.

### Components

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cartridge_(firearms)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Components")]

A modern cartridge consists of four main components: the *case*, the *projectile*, the *propellant*, and the *primer*.

#### Case

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cartridge_(firearms)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Case")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Many_bullets.jpg/330px-Many_bullets.jpg)](/wiki/File:Many_bullets.jpg)

(Left) Three non-bottlenecked cartridges ([9×19mm Parabellum](/wiki/9%C3%9719mm_Parabellum "9×19mm Parabellum"), [.40 S&W](/wiki/.40_S%26W ".40 S&W") and [.45 ACP](/wiki/.45_ACP ".45 ACP"))  
(Center) Three bottlenecked cartridges ([FN 5.7×28mm](/wiki/FN_5.7%C3%9728mm "FN 5.7×28mm"), [5.56×45mm NATO](/wiki/5.56%C3%9745mm_NATO "5.56×45mm NATO") and [.300 Winchester Magnum](/wiki/.300_Winchester_Magnum ".300 Winchester Magnum"))   
(Right) Two [polymer-cased](/wiki/Polymer-cased_ammunition "Polymer-cased ammunition") [12-gauge](/wiki/12-gauge "12-gauge") [shotshells](/wiki/Shotshell "Shotshell")

The main defining component of the cartridge is the case, which gives the cartridge its shape and serves as the integrating [housing](/wiki/Housing_(engineering) "Housing (engineering)") for other functional components. It acts as a container for the propellant powders and also serves as a protective [shell](/wiki/Shell_(structure) "Shell (structure)") against the elements; it attaches the projectile either at the front end of the cartridge ([bullets](/wiki/Bullet "Bullet") for [pistols](/wiki/Pistol "Pistol"), [submachine guns](/wiki/Submachine_gun "Submachine gun"), [rifles](/wiki/Rifle "Rifle"), and [machine guns](/wiki/Machine_gun "Machine gun")) or inside of the cartridge ([wadding](/wiki/Wadding "Wadding")/[sabot](/wiki/Sabot_(firearms) "Sabot (firearms)") containing either a quantity of [shot (pellets)](/wiki/Shot_(pellet) "Shot (pellet)") or an individual [slug](/wiki/Shotgun_slug "Shotgun slug") for [shotguns](/wiki/Shotgun "Shotgun")), and aligns it with the [barrel bore](/wiki/Gun_barrel#Bore "Gun barrel") to the front; it holds the [primer](/wiki/Primer_(firearms) "Primer (firearms)") at the back end, which receives an impact from a [firing pin](/wiki/Firing_pin "Firing pin") and is responsible for igniting the main propellant charge inside the case.

While historically [paper](/wiki/Cartridge_paper "Cartridge paper") had been used in the [earliest cartridges](/wiki/Paper_cartridge "Paper cartridge"), almost all modern cartridges use [metallic casing](/wiki/Metallic_cartridge "Metallic cartridge"). The modern metallic case can either be "bottleneck", whose frontal portion near the end opening (known as the "case *neck*") has a noticeably smaller [diameter](/wiki/Diameter "Diameter") than the main part of the case ("case *body*"), with a noticeably angled slope ("case *shoulder*") in between, or "straight-walled", where there is no narrowed neck and the whole case looks [cylindrical](/wiki/Cylindrical "Cylindrical"). The case shape is meant to match exactly to the [chamber](/wiki/Chamber_(firearms) "Chamber (firearms)") of the gun that fires it, and the "neck", "shoulder", and "body" of a bottleneck cartridge have corresponding counterparts in the chamber known as the "chamber neck", "chamber shoulder", and "chamber body".

Some cartridges, like the [.470 Capstick](/wiki/.470_Capstick ".470 Capstick"), have what is known as a "ghost shoulder", a very slightly protruding shoulder, and can be viewed as a something between a bottleneck and straight-walled case. A ghost shoulder, rather than a continuous taper on the case wall, helps the cartridge to line up concentrically with the bore axis, contributing to accuracy. The front opening of the case neck, which receives and fastens the bullet via [crimping](/wiki/Crimp_(joining) "Crimp (joining)"), is known as the *case mouth*. The closed-off rear end of the case body, which holds the [primer](/wiki/Primer_(firearms) "Primer (firearms)") and technically is the case *base*, is called the *case head*, as it is the most prominent and frequently widest part of the case. There is a circumferential [flange](/wiki/Flange "Flange") at the case head called a *[rim](/wiki/Rim_(firearms) "Rim (firearms)")*, which provides a lip for the [extractor](/wiki/Extractor_(firearms) "Extractor (firearms)") to engage. Depending on whether and how the rim protrudes beyond the maximum case body diameter, the case can be classified as either "rimmed", "semi-rimmed", "rimless", "rebated", or "belted".

The shape of a bottleneck cartridge case (e.g. body diameter, shoulder slant angle and position, and neck length) also affects the amount of attainable pressure inside the case, which in turn influences the [accelerative](/wiki/Accelerative "Accelerative") capacity of the projectile. [Wildcat cartridges](/wiki/Wildcat_cartridge "Wildcat cartridge") are often made by reshaping the case of an existing cartridge. Straight-sided cartridges are less prone to rupturing than [tapered cartridges](/wiki/Tapering_(firearms) "Tapering (firearms)"), in particular with higher pressure propellant when used in blowback-operated firearms.

In addition to case shape, [rifle cartridges](/wiki/Rifle_cartridge "Rifle cartridge") can be grouped according to the case dimensions of a cartridge; this usually refers to the cartridge's overall length (COL), which in turn dictates the minimal [receiver](/wiki/Receiver_(firearms) "Receiver (firearms)") size and operating space ([bolt](/wiki/Bolt_(firearms) "Bolt (firearms)") travel) needed by the [action](/wiki/Action_(firearms) "Action (firearms)"). They can be grouped into the "mini-action", "short-action", "long-action" ("standard-action"), or "[magnum](/wiki/Magnum_cartridge "Magnum cartridge")-action" categories.

* *Mini-action* cartridges are usually [intermediate rifle cartridges](/wiki/Intermediate_cartridge "Intermediate cartridge") with a COL of 57 [mm](/wiki/Millimetre "Millimetre") (2.25 [in](/wiki/Inch "Inch")) or shorter in length, which is most commonly exemplified by the [.223 Remington](/wiki/.223_Remington ".223 Remington");
* *Short-action* cartridges are usually [full-powered rifle cartridges](/wiki/Fully_powered_cartridge "Fully powered cartridge") with a COL between 57 and 71 mm (2.25 and 2.8 in), which is most commonly exemplified by the [.308 Winchester](/wiki/.308_Winchester ".308 Winchester");
* *Long-action* ("standard-action") cartridges are usually traditional full-powered rifle cartridges with a COL between 71 and 85 mm (2.8 and 3.34 in), which is most commonly exemplified by the [.30-06 Springfield](/wiki/.30-06_Springfield ".30-06 Springfield");
* *Magnum-action* cartridges are rifle cartridges that are longer, wider, and more powerful than traditional long-action rifle cartridges, with a COL between 85 and 91 mm (3.34 and 3.6 in), including some of the long-action cartridges with a case head larger than 13 mm (.50 in) diameter, which is most commonly exemplified by the [.375 Holland & Holland Magnum](/wiki/.375_Holland_%26_Holland_Magnum ".375 Holland & Holland Magnum").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The most popular material used to make cartridge cases is [brass](/wiki/Brass "Brass") due to its good [corrosion resistance](/wiki/Corrosion_resistance "Corrosion resistance"). The head of a brass case can be work-hardened to withstand the high pressures, and allow for manipulation via extraction and ejection without rupturing. The neck and body portion of a brass case is easily annealed to make the case [ductile](/wiki/Ductile "Ductile") enough to allow reshaping so that it can be [handloaded](/wiki/Handloading "Handloading") many times, and [fire forming](/wiki/Fire_forming "Fire forming") can help [accurize](/wiki/Accurize "Accurize") the shooting.

Steel casing is used in some [plinking](/wiki/Plinking "Plinking") ammunition, as well as in some military training ammunition (mostly from the [former Soviet republics](/wiki/Former_Soviet_republics "Former Soviet republics") of Armenia, Azerbaijan, Belarus, Estonia, Georgia, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Latvia, Lithuania, Moldova, Russia, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, Ukraine, and Uzbekistan), along with Russia and China.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Steel is less expensive to make than brass, but it is far less corrosion-resistant and not feasible to reuse and reload. Military forces typically consider [service](/wiki/Service_rifle "Service rifle") [small arms](/wiki/Small_arms "Small arms") cartridge cases to be disposable, single-use devices. However, the mass of the cartridges can affect how much ammunition a soldier can carry, so the lighter steel cases do have a [logistic](/wiki/Military_logistics "Military logistics") advantage.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Conversely, steel is more susceptible to corrosion, so all such cases are varnished or otherwise sealed against the elements. One downside caused by the increased strength of steel in the neck of these cases (compared to the annealed neck of a brass case) is that [propellant](/wiki/Propellant "Propellant") gas can blow back past the neck and leak into the chamber. Constituents of these gases condense on the (relatively cold) chamber wall, and this solid propellant residue can make extraction of fired cases difficult. This is less of a problem for small arms of the former [Warsaw Pact](/wiki/Warsaw_Pact "Warsaw Pact") nations, which were designed with much looser chamber [tolerances](/wiki/Engineering_tolerance "Engineering tolerance") than [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") weapons.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Aluminum](/wiki/Aluminium "Aluminium")-cased cartridges are available commercially. These are generally not reloaded, as aluminum [fatigues](/wiki/Fatigue_(material) "Fatigue (material)") easily during firing and resizing. Some calibers also have non-standard primer sizes to discourage reloaders from attempting to reuse these cases.

[Plastic cases](/wiki/Polymer-cased_ammunition "Polymer-cased ammunition") are commonly used in [shotgun shells](/wiki/Shotgun_shell "Shotgun shell"), and some manufacturers offer [polymer](/wiki/Polymer "Polymer")-cased [centerfire](/wiki/Centerfire "Centerfire") pistol and rifle cartridges.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

#### Projectile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cartridge_(firearms)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Projectile")]

Main articles: [Bullet](/wiki/Bullet "Bullet"), [Shot (pellet)](/wiki/Shot_(pellet) "Shot (pellet)"), and [Shotgun slug](/wiki/Shotgun_slug "Shotgun slug")

As firearms are projectile weapons, the projectile is the effector component of the cartridge, and is actually responsible for reaching, impacting, and exerting damage onto a target. The word "projectile" is an [umbrella term](/wiki/Umbrella_term "Umbrella term") that describes any type of [kinetic object](/wiki/Projectile "Projectile") launched into [ballistic flight](/wiki/Projectile_motion "Projectile motion"), but due to the ubiquity of [rifled](/wiki/Rifled "Rifled") firearms shooting bullets, the term has become somewhat a technical [synonym](/wiki/Synonym "Synonym") for bullets among [handloaders](/wiki/Handloading "Handloading"). The projectile's motion in flight is known as its [external ballistics](/wiki/External_ballistics "External ballistics"), and its behavior upon impacting an object is known as its [terminal ballistics](/wiki/Terminal_ballistics "Terminal ballistics").

A bullet can be made of virtually anything (see [below](#Projectile_design_types)), but [lead](/wiki/Lead "Lead") is the traditional material of choice because of its high density, [malleability](/wiki/Malleability "Malleability"), [ductility](/wiki/Ductility "Ductility"), and low [cost of production](/wiki/Cost_of_production "Cost of production"). However, at speeds greater than 300 m/s (980 ft/s), pure lead will melt more and deposit [fouling](/wiki/Fouling "Fouling") in rifled bores at an ever-increasing rate. [Alloying](/wiki/Alloying "Alloying") the lead with a small percentage of [tin](/wiki/Tin "Tin") or [antimony](/wiki/Antimony "Antimony") can reduce such fouling, but grows less effective as velocities are increased. A cup made of harder metal (e.g. copper), called a *gas check*, is often placed at the base of a lead bullet to decrease lead deposits by protecting the rear of the bullet against melting when fired at higher pressures, but this too does not work at higher velocities. A modern solution is to cover the bare lead in a protective [powder coat](/wiki/Powder_coat "Powder coat"), as seen in some rimfire ammunition. Another solution is to encase a lead core within a thin exterior layer of harder metal (e.g. [gilding metal](/wiki/Gilding_metal "Gilding metal"), [cupronickel](/wiki/Cupronickel "Cupronickel"), other [copper alloys](/wiki/Copper_alloys "Copper alloys"), or steel), known as a *[jacketing](/wiki/Jacketing "Jacketing")*. In the modern day, steel, [bismuth](/wiki/Bismuth "Bismuth"), [tungsten](/wiki/Tungsten "Tungsten"), and other exotic alloys are sometimes used to replace lead and prevent [release of toxicity](/wiki/Lead_poisoning "Lead poisoning") into the environment. In [armor-piercing bullets](/wiki/Armor-piercing_bullet "Armor-piercing bullet"), very hard and high-density materials such as [hardened steel](/wiki/Hardened_steel "Hardened steel"), [tungsten](/wiki/Tungsten "Tungsten"), [tungsten carbide](/wiki/Tungsten_carbide "Tungsten carbide"), or [depleted uranium](/wiki/Depleted_uranium "Depleted uranium") are used for the penetrator core.

[Non-lethal](/wiki/Non-lethal_weapon "Non-lethal weapon") projectiles with very limited [penetrative](/wiki/Penetration_(weaponry) "Penetration (weaponry)") and [stopping power](/wiki/Stopping_power "Stopping power") are sometimes used in [riot control](/wiki/Riot_control "Riot control") or training situations, where killing or even wounding a target at all would be undesirable. Such projectiles are usually made from softer and lower-density materials, such as [plastic](/wiki/Plastic_bullet "Plastic bullet") or [rubber](/wiki/Rubber_bullet "Rubber bullet"). [Wax bullets](/wiki/Wax_bullet "Wax bullet") (such as those used in [simunition](/wiki/Simunition "Simunition") training) are occasionally used for force-on-force [tactical training](/wiki/Tactical_engagement_simulation "Tactical engagement simulation"), and [pistol dueling](/wiki/Pistol_dueling "Pistol dueling") with wax bullets used to be a [competitive](/wiki/Shooting_sport "Shooting sport") [Olympic sport](/wiki/Olympic_sport "Olympic sport") prior to [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").

For [smoothbore](/wiki/Smoothbore "Smoothbore") weapons such as shotguns, small metallic balls known as [shot](/wiki/Shot_(pellet) "Shot (pellet)") are typically used, usually contained inside a semi-flexible, cup-like [sabot](/wiki/Sabot_(firearms) "Sabot (firearms)") called "[wadding](/wiki/Wadding "Wadding")". When fired, the wadding is launched from the gun as a payload-carrying projectile, loosens and opens itself up after exiting the barrel, and then [inertially](/wiki/Inertia "Inertia") releases the contained shots as a hail of sub-projectiles. Shotgun shots are usually made from bare lead, though copper/[zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc")–[coated](/wiki/Total_metal_jacket "Total metal jacket") steel balls (such as those used by [BB guns](/wiki/BB_gun "BB gun")) can also be used. Lead pollution of wetlands has led to the [BASC](/wiki/British_Association_for_Shooting_and_Conservation "British Association for Shooting and Conservation") and other organizations campaigning for the phasing out of traditional lead shot.[[7]](#cite_note-7) There are also unconventional projectile fillings such as bundled [flechettes](/wiki/Flechette#Shotguns "Flechette"), [rubber balls](/wiki/Riot_gun#Rubber_buckshot "Riot gun"), [rock salt](/wiki/Rock_salt "Rock salt") and [magnesium](/wiki/Magnesium "Magnesium") shards, as well as [non-lethal](/wiki/Non-lethal_weapon "Non-lethal weapon") [specialty projectiles](/wiki/Shotgun#Specialty_ammunition "Shotgun") such as [rubber slugs](/wiki/Baton_round "Baton round") and [bean bag rounds](/wiki/Bean_bag_round "Bean bag round"). Solid projectiles (e.g. [slugs](/wiki/Shotgun_slug "Shotgun slug"), [baton rounds](/wiki/Baton_round "Baton round"), etc.) are also shot while contained within a wadding, as the wadding [obturates](/wiki/Obturate "Obturate") the bore better and typically [slides](/wiki/Sliding_(motion) "Sliding (motion)") less frictionally within the barrel.

#### Propellant

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cartridge_(firearms)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Propellant")]

Main article: [Firearm propellant](/wiki/Firearm_propellant "Firearm propellant")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Powder_Samples.jpg/250px-Powder_Samples.jpg)](/wiki/File:Powder_Samples.jpg)

Smokeless powders used for [handloading](/wiki/Handloading "Handloading")

When a propellant is ignited and begins to [combust](/wiki/Combustion "Combustion"), the resulting [chemical reaction](/wiki/Chemical_reaction "Chemical reaction") releases the [chemical energy](/wiki/Chemical_energy "Chemical energy") stored within. At the same time, a significant amount of gaseous [products](/wiki/Product_(chemistry) "Product (chemistry)") are released, which are highly energetic due to the [exothermic](/wiki/Exothermic "Exothermic") nature of the reaction. These combustion gases become highly pressurized in a confined space—such as the cartridge casing (reinforced by the [chamber](/wiki/Chamber_(firearms) "Chamber (firearms)") wall) occluded from the front by the projectile (bullet, or [wadding](/wiki/Wadding "Wadding") containing [shots](/wiki/Shot_(pellet) "Shot (pellet)")/[slug](/wiki/Shotgun_slug "Shotgun slug")) and from behind by the [primer](/wiki/Primer_(firearms) "Primer (firearms)") (supported by the [bolt](/wiki/Bolt_(firearms) "Bolt (firearms)")/[breechblock](/wiki/Breechblock "Breechblock")). When the pressure builds up high enough to overcome the [crimp](/wiki/Crimp_(joining) "Crimp (joining)") friction between the projectile and the case, the projectile separates from the case and gets *propelled* down the [gun barrel](/wiki/Gun_barrel "Gun barrel"), imparting high [kinetic energy](/wiki/Kinetic_energy "Kinetic energy") from the propellant gases and accelerating the projectile to its [muzzle velocity](/wiki/Muzzle_velocity "Muzzle velocity"). The projectile motion driven by the propellant inside the gun is known as the [internal ballistics](/wiki/Internal_ballistics "Internal ballistics").

#### Primer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cartridge_(firearms)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Primer")]

Main article: [Primer (firearm)](/wiki/Primer_(firearm) "Primer (firearm)")

See also: [Percussion cap](/wiki/Percussion_cap "Percussion cap")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Percussion_caps.jpg/250px-Percussion_caps.jpg)](/wiki/File:Percussion_caps.jpg)

Percussion caps, the precursor of modern primers

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Centerfire_%26_rimfire_ignition.gif/250px-Centerfire_%26_rimfire_ignition.gif)](/wiki/File:Centerfire_%26_rimfire_ignition.gif)

Comparison of primer ignition between centerfire (left two) and rimfire (right) ammunitions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Berdan_vs_boxer2.jpg/250px-Berdan_vs_boxer2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Berdan_vs_boxer2.jpg)

[Flash hole](/wiki/Flash_hole "Flash hole") profiles on Berdan (left) and Boxer (right) primers.

Because the main propellant charge is located deep inside the [gun barrel](/wiki/Gun_barrel "Gun barrel") and thus impractical to be directly lighted from the outside, an intermediate is needed to relay the [ignition](/wiki/Fire_making "Fire making"). In the earliest [black powder](/wiki/Black_powder "Black powder") [muzzleloaders](/wiki/Muzzleloader "Muzzleloader"), a [fuse](/wiki/Fuse_(explosives) "Fuse (explosives)") was used to direct a small flame through a [touch hole](/wiki/Touch_hole "Touch hole") into the barrel, which was slow and subjected to disturbance from environmental conditions. The next evolution was to have a small separate charge of finer gunpowder poured into a [flash pan](/wiki/Flash_pan "Flash pan"), where it could start a "priming" ignition by an external source, when ignited the flame passed through a small hole in the side of the barrel to ignite the main gunpowder charge.

The last evolution was to use a small [metallic cap](/wiki/Percussion_cap "Percussion cap") filled with a shock sensitive explosive compound that would ignite with a [hammer](/wiki/Hammer_(firearms) "Hammer (firearms)") strike. The source of ignition could be a burning [slow match](/wiki/Slow_match "Slow match") ([matchlock](/wiki/Matchlock "Matchlock")) placed onto a [touch hole](/wiki/Touch_hole "Touch hole"), a piece of [pyrite](/wiki/Pyrite "Pyrite") ([wheellock](/wiki/Wheellock "Wheellock"))/[flint](/wiki/Flint "Flint") ([flintlock](/wiki/Flintlock "Flintlock")) striking a steel [frizzen](/wiki/Frizzen "Frizzen"), or a [shock-sensitive](/wiki/Shock_sensitivity "Shock sensitivity") brass or copper percussion cap ([caplock](/wiki/Caplock "Caplock")) placed over a conical-shaped cone piece with a hollow pipe to create [sparks](/wiki/Spark_(fire) "Spark (fire)"). When the primer powder starts combusting, the flame is transferred through an internal touch hole called a *flash hole* to provide [activation energy](/wiki/Activation_energy "Activation energy") for the main powder charge in the barrel. The disadvantage Is that the flash pan cAN still be exposed to the outside, making it difficult (or even impossible) to fire the gun in rainy or humid conditions as wet gunpowder burns poorly.

After [Edward Charles Howard](/wiki/Edward_Charles_Howard "Edward Charles Howard") discovered [fulminates](/wiki/Fulminate "Fulminate") in 1800[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) and the patent by [Reverend](/wiki/Reverend "Reverend") [Alexander John Forsyth](/wiki/Alexander_John_Forsyth "Alexander John Forsyth") expired in 1807,[[10]](#cite_note-Fadala2006-10) [Joseph Manton](/wiki/Joseph_Manton "Joseph Manton") invented the precursor [percussion cap](/wiki/Percussion_cap "Percussion cap") in 1814,[[11]](#cite_note-11) which was further developed in 1822 by the English-born American artist [Joshua Shaw](/wiki/Joshua_Shaw "Joshua Shaw"),[[12]](#cite_note-12) and [caplock](/wiki/Caplock "Caplock") [fowling pieces](/wiki/Fowling_piece "Fowling piece") appeared in [Regency era](/wiki/Regency_era "Regency era") England. These guns used a [spring](/wiki/Spring_(device) "Spring (device)")-loaded [hammer](/wiki/Hammer_(firearms) "Hammer (firearms)") to strike a percussion cap placed over a [conical](/wiki/Conical "Conical") "nipple", which served as both an "[anvil](/wiki/Anvil "Anvil")" against the hammer strike and a transfer port for the sparks created by crushing the cap, and was easier and quicker to load, more resilient to weather conditions, and more reliable than the preceding flintlocks.[[10]](#cite_note-Fadala2006-10)

Modern primers are basically improved percussion caps with [shock-sensitive](/wiki/Shock_sensitivity "Shock sensitivity") chemicals (e.g. [lead styphnate](/wiki/Lead_styphnate "Lead styphnate")) enclosed in a small button-shaped capsule. In the early [paper cartridges](/wiki/Paper_cartridge "Paper cartridge"), invented not long after the percussion cap, the primer was located deep inside the cartridge just behind the bullet, requiring a very thin and elongated [firing pin](/wiki/Firing_pin "Firing pin") to pierce the paper casing. Such guns were known as [needle guns](/wiki/Needle_gun "Needle gun"), [the most famous of which](/wiki/Dreyse_needle_gun "Dreyse needle gun") was decisive in the [Prussian](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") victory over the [Austrians](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") at [Königgrätz](/wiki/Battle_of_K%C3%B6niggr%C3%A4tz "Battle of Königgrätz") in 1866. After the metallic cartridge was invented, the primer was relocated backward to the base of the case, either at the center of the case head ([centerfire](/wiki/Centerfire "Centerfire")), inside the rim ([rimfire](/wiki/Rimfire_ammunition "Rimfire ammunition")), inside a cup-like concavity of the case base (cupfire), in a pin-shaped sideways projection ([pinfire](/wiki/Pinfire "Pinfire")), in a lip-like [flange](/wiki/Flange "Flange") ([lipfire](/wiki/Ethan_Allen_(armsmaker)#Cartridge_pistols "Ethan Allen (armsmaker)")), or in a small nipple-like bulge at the case base ([teat-fire](/wiki/Teat-fire "Teat-fire")). Today, only the centerfire and rimfire have survived as the mainstream primer designs, while the pinfire also still exists but only in rare novelty miniature guns and a few very small [blank cartridges](/wiki/Blank_cartridge "Blank cartridge") designed as noisemakers.

In rimfire ammunitions, the primer compound is [moulded](/wiki/Injection_moulding "Injection moulding") integrally into the interior of the protruding [case rim](/wiki/Rim_(firearms) "Rim (firearms)"), which is crushed between the firing pin and the edge of the barrel breech (serving as the "anvil"). These ammunitions are thus not [reloadable](/wiki/Handloading "Handloading"), and are usually on the lower end of the [power](/wiki/Muzzle_energy "Muzzle energy") spectrum, although due to the low [manufacturing cost](/wiki/Manufacturing_cost "Manufacturing cost") some of them (e.g. [.22 Long Rifle](/wiki/.22_Long_Rifle ".22 Long Rifle")) are among the most popular and prolific ammunitions currently being used.

Centerfire primers are a separately manufactured component, seated into a central recess at the case base known as the *primer pocket*, and have two types: Berdan and Boxer. Berdan primers, patent by American inventor [Hiram Berdan](/wiki/Hiram_Berdan "Hiram Berdan") in 1866, are a simple capsule, and the corresponding case has two small flash holes with a bulged bar in between, which serves as the "anvil" for the primer. Boxer primers, patented by [Royal Artillery](/wiki/Royal_Artillery "Royal Artillery") colonel [Edward Mounier Boxer](/wiki/Edward_Mounier_Boxer "Edward Mounier Boxer") also in 1866, are more complex and have an internal tripedal "anvil" built into the primer itself, and the corresponding case has only a single large central flash hole. Commercially, Boxer primers dominate the [handloader](/wiki/Handloading "Handloading") market due to the ease of depriming and the ability to transfer sparks more efficiently.

Due to their small size and charge load, primers lack the power to shoot out the projectile by themselves, but can still put out enough energy to separate the bullet from the casing and push it partway into the barrel – a dangerous condition called a [squib load](/wiki/Squib_load "Squib load"). Firing a fresh cartridge behind a squib load obstructing the barrel will generate dangerously high pressure, leading to a [catastrophic failure](/wiki/Catastrophic_failure "Catastrophic failure") and potentially causing severe injuries when the gun blows apart in the shooter's hands. Actor [Brandon Lee](/wiki/Brandon_Lee "Brandon Lee")'s [infamous accidental death in 1993](/wiki/The_Crow_(1994_film)#Brandon_Lee's_death "The Crow (1994 film)") was believed to be caused by an undetected squib that was dislodged and shot out by a [blank](/wiki/Blank_(cartridge) "Blank (cartridge)").

### Manufacturing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cartridge_(firearms)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Manufacturing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/0.30-30_Winchester_case%2C_stages_in_the_drawing_process%2C_book%3B_Cartridge_Manufacture_%281916%29%2C_author%3B_Douglas_T._Hamilton.png/500px-0.30-30_Winchester_case%2C_stages_in_the_drawing_process%2C_book%3B_Cartridge_Manufacture_%281916%29%2C_author%3B_Douglas_T._Hamilton.png)](/wiki/File:0.30-30_Winchester_case,_stages_in_the_drawing_process,_book;_Cartridge_Manufacture_(1916),_author;_Douglas_T._Hamilton.png)

.30–30 Winchester case, stages in the drawing process, book; from Hamilton[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13)

Beginning in the 1860s, early metallic cartridges (e. g. for the [Montigny mitrailleuse](/wiki/Montigny_mitrailleuse "Montigny mitrailleuse")[[14]](#cite_note-14) or the [Snider–Enfield](/wiki/Snider%E2%80%93Enfield "Snider–Enfield") rifle[[15]](#cite_note-15)) were produced similarly to the paper cartridges, with sides made from thick paper, but with copper (later brass) foil supporting the base of the cartridge and some more details in it holding the primer. In the 1870s, brass foil covered all of the cartridge, and the technology to make solid cases, in which the metallic cartridges described below were developed, but before the 1880s, it was far too expensive and time-consuming for mass production[[16]](#cite_note-16) and the metallurgy was not yet perfected.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

To manufacture cases for cartridges, a sheet of brass is punched into disks. These disks go through a series of [drawing dies](/wiki/Deep_drawing "Deep drawing"). The disks are [annealed](/wiki/Annealing_(metallurgy) "Annealing (metallurgy)") and washed before moving to the next series of dies. The brass needs to be annealed to remove the work-hardening in the material and make the brass malleable again ready for the next series of dies.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13)

Manufacturing bullet jackets is similar to making brass cases: there is a series of drawing steps with annealing and washing.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13)

### Specifications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cartridge_(firearms)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Specifications")]

Critical cartridge specifications include neck size, bullet weight and [caliber](/wiki/Caliber "Caliber"), maximum pressure, [headspace](/wiki/Headspace_(firearms) "Headspace (firearms)"), overall length, case body diameter and taper, shoulder design, [rim type](/wiki/Rim_(firearms) "Rim (firearms)"), etc. Generally, every characteristic of a specific cartridge type is tightly controlled and few types are interchangeable in any way. Exceptions do exist but generally, these are only where a shorter cylindrical rimmed cartridge can be used in a longer chamber, (e.g., .22 Short in .22 Long Rifle chamber, .32 H&R Magnum in .327 Federal Magnum chamber, and .38 Special in a .357 Magnum chamber). [Centerfire](/wiki/Centerfire_ammunition "Centerfire ammunition") primer type (Boxer or Berdan, see below) is interchangeable, although not in the same case. Deviation in any of these specifications can result in firearm damage and, in some instances, injury or death. Similarly, the use of the wrong type of cartridge in any given gun can damage the gun, or cause bodily injury.

Cartridge specifications are determined by several standards organizations, including [SAAMI](/wiki/SAAMI "SAAMI") in the United States, and [C.I.P.](/wiki/C.I.P. "C.I.P.") in many European states. NATO also performs [its own tests](/wiki/NATO_EPVAT_testing "NATO EPVAT testing") for military cartridges for its member nations; due to differences in testing methods, NATO cartridges ([headstamped](/wiki/Headstamp "Headstamp") with the NATO cross) may present an unsafe combination when loaded into a weapon chambered for a cartridge certified by one of the other testing bodies.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Bullet diameter is measured either as a fraction of an inch (usually in 1/100 or in 1/1000) or in millimeters. Cartridge case length can also be designated in inches or millimeters.

Historic British cartridges

[Paper cartridges](/wiki/Paper_cartridge "Paper cartridge") have been in use for centuries, with a number of sources dating their usage as far back as the late 14th and early 15th centuries. Historians note their use by soldiers of [Christian I, Elector of Saxony](/wiki/Christian_I,_Elector_of_Saxony "Christian I, Elector of Saxony") and [his son](/wiki/Christian_II,_Elector_of_Saxony "Christian II, Elector of Saxony") in the late 16th century,[[19]](#cite_note-greener-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) while the [Dresden Armoury](/wiki/Dresden_Armoury "Dresden Armoury") has evidence dating their use to 1591.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[19]](#cite_note-greener-19) Capo Bianco wrote in 1597 that paper cartridges had long been in use by Neapolitan soldiers. Their use became widespread by the 17th century.[[19]](#cite_note-greener-19) The 1586 round consisted of a charge of [powder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder") and a bullet in a paper cartridge. Thick paper is still known as "[cartridge paper](/wiki/Cartridge_paper "Cartridge paper")" from its use in these cartridges.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Another source states the cartridge appeared in 1590.[[23]](#cite_note-ArmyTM-23) King [Gustavus Adolphus of Sweden](/wiki/Gustavus_Adolphus_of_Sweden "Gustavus Adolphus of Sweden") had his troops use cartridges in the 1600s.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The paper formed a cylinder with twisted ends; the ball was at one end, and the measured powder filled the rest.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

This cartridge was used with [muzzle-loading](/wiki/Muzzle-loading "Muzzle-loading") military firearms, probably more often than for sporting shooting, the base of the cartridge being ripped or bitten off by the soldier, the powder poured into the barrel, and the paper and bullet rammed down the barrel.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In the US Civil War era cartridge, the paper was supposed to be discarded, but soldiers often used it as a wad.[[27]](#cite_note-27) To ignite the charge an additional step was required where a finer-grained powder called priming powder was poured into the pan of the gun to be ignited by the firing mechanism.

The evolving nature of warfare required a firearm that could load and fire more rapidly, resulting in the flintlock musket (and later the Baker rifle), in which the pan was covered by furrowed steel. This was struck by the flint and fired the gun. In the course of loading, a pinch of powder from the cartridge would be placed into the pan as priming, before the rest of the cartridge was rammed down the barrel, providing charge and wadding.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Later developments rendered this method of priming unnecessary, as, in loading, a portion of the charge of powder passed from the barrel through the vent into the pan, where it was held by the cover and hammer. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The next important advance in the method of ignition was the introduction of the copper [percussion cap](/wiki/Percussion_cap "Percussion cap"). This was only generally applied to the British military musket (the [Brown Bess](/wiki/Brown_Bess "Brown Bess")) in 1842, a quarter of a century after the invention of percussion powder and after an elaborate government test at Woolwich in 1834. The invention that made the percussion cap possible was patented by the Rev. [A. J. Forsyth](/wiki/Alexander_John_Forsyth "Alexander John Forsyth") in 1807 and consisted of priming with a fulminating powder made of [potassium chlorate](/wiki/Potassium_chlorate "Potassium chlorate"), sulfur, and charcoal, which ignited by concussion. This invention was gradually developed, and used, first in a steel cap, and then in a copper cap, by various gunmakers and private individuals before coming into general military use nearly thirty years later.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The alteration of the military flint-lock to the percussion [musket](/wiki/Musket "Musket") was easily accomplished by replacing the powder pan with a perforated nipple and by replacing the cock or hammer that held the flint with a smaller hammer that had a hollow to fit on the nipple when released by the trigger. The shooter placed a percussion cap (now made of three parts of [potassium chlorate](/wiki/Potassium_chlorate "Potassium chlorate"), two of [fulminate of mercury](/wiki/Mercury_fulminate "Mercury fulminate") and powdered glass) on the nipple. The detonating cap thus invented and adopted brought about the invention of the modern cartridge case, and rendered possible the general adoption of the [breech-loading](/wiki/Breech-loading_weapon "Breech-loading weapon") principle for all varieties of rifles, shotguns, and [pistols](/wiki/Pistol "Pistol"). This greatly streamlined the reloading procedure and paved the way for semi- and full-automatic firearms.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

However, this big leap forward came at a price: it introduced an extra component into each round—the cartridge case—which had to be removed before the gun could be reloaded. While a flintlock, for example, is immediately ready to reload once it has been fired, adopting brass cartridge cases brought in the problems of extraction and ejection. The mechanism of a modern gun must not only load and fire the piece but also provide a method of removing the spent case, which might require just as many added moving parts. Many [malfunctions](/wiki/Firearm_malfunction "Firearm malfunction") occur during this process, either through a failure to extract a case properly from the chamber or by allowing the extracted case to jam the action. Nineteenth-century inventors were reluctant to accept this added complication and experimented with a variety of [caseless](/wiki/Caseless "Caseless") or self-consuming cartridges before finally accepting that the advantages of brass cases far outweighed this one drawback.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Integrated cartridges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cartridge_(firearms)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Integrated cartridges")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Chassepot_paper_cartridge.jpg/250px-Chassepot_paper_cartridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chassepot_paper_cartridge.jpg)

[Chassepot](/wiki/Chassepot "Chassepot") paper cartridge (1866)

The first integrated cartridge was developed in Paris in 1808 by the Swiss gunsmith [Jean Samuel Pauly](/wiki/Jean_Samuel_Pauly "Jean Samuel Pauly") in association with French gunsmith [François Prélat](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Pr%C3%A9lat "François Prélat"). Pauly created the first fully self-contained cartridges:[[30]](#cite_note-Smyth-30) the cartridges incorporated a copper base with integrated [mercury fulminate](/wiki/Mercury_fulminate "Mercury fulminate") primer powder (the major innovation of Pauly), a round bullet and either brass or paper casing.[[31]](#cite_note-sil.si.edu-31)[[32]](#cite_note-Pauly-32) The cartridge was loaded through the breech and fired with a needle. The needle-activated centerfire [breech-loading](/wiki/Breech-loading "Breech-loading") gun would become a major feature of firearms thereafter.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Pauly made an improved version, protected by a patent, on 29 September 1812.[[30]](#cite_note-Smyth-30)

Probably no invention connected with firearms has wrought such changes in the principle of gun construction as those effected by the "expansive cartridge case". This invention has completely revolutionized the art of gun making, has been successfully applied to all descriptions of firearms and has produced a new and important industry: that of cartridge manufacture. Its essential feature is preventing gas from escaping the breech when the gun is fired, by means of an expansive cartridge case containing its own means of ignition. Previous to this invention shotguns and sporting rifles were loaded by means of [powder flasks](/wiki/Powder_flask "Powder flask") and shot bags or flasks, bullets, wads, and copper caps, all carried separately. One of the earliest efficient modern cartridge cases was the [pinfire cartridge](/wiki/Pinfire_cartridge "Pinfire cartridge"), developed by French gunsmith [Casimir Lefaucheux](/wiki/Casimir_Lefaucheux "Casimir Lefaucheux") in 1836.[[34]](#cite_note-Pistols-34) It consisted of a thin weak shell made of brass and paper that expanded from the force of the explosion. This fit perfectly in the barrel and thus formed an efficient gas check. A small percussion cap was placed in the middle of the base of the cartridge and was ignited by means of a brass pin projecting from the side and struck by the hammer. This pin also afforded the means of extracting the cartridge case. This cartridge was introduced in England by Lang, of Cockspur Street, London, about 1845.

In the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") (1861–1865) a breech-loading rifle, the [Sharps](/wiki/Sharps_rifle "Sharps rifle"), was introduced and produced in large numbers. It could be loaded with either a ball or a [paper cartridge](/wiki/Paper_cartridge "Paper cartridge"). After that war, many were converted to the use of metal cartridges. The development by [Smith & Wesson](/wiki/Smith_%26_Wesson "Smith & Wesson") (among many others) of revolver handguns that used metal cartridges helped establish cartridge firearms as the standard in the United States by the late 1860s and early 1870s, although many continue to use percussion revolvers well after that.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Modern metallic cartridges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cartridge_(firearms)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Modern metallic cartridges")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Snider-Martini-Enfield_Cartridges.JPG/250px-Snider-Martini-Enfield_Cartridges.JPG)](/wiki/File:Snider-Martini-Enfield_Cartridges.JPG)

(From Left to Right): A [.577 Snider](/wiki/.577_Snider ".577 Snider") cartridge (1867), a [.577/450 Martini-Henry](/wiki/.577/450_Martini-Henry ".577/450 Martini-Henry") cartridge (1871), a later drawn brass .577/450 [Martini-Henry](/wiki/Martini-Henry "Martini-Henry") cartridge, and a [.303 British](/wiki/.303_British ".303 British") Mk VII SAA Ball cartridge.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Fusil_Gras_M80_1874_metallic_cartridge.jpg/250px-Fusil_Gras_M80_1874_metallic_cartridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fusil_Gras_M80_1874_metallic_cartridge.jpg)

French Army [Fusil Gras mle 1874](/wiki/Fusil_Gras_mle_1874 "Fusil Gras mle 1874") metallic cartridge.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/8mm_Lebel_Tarbes-01.jpg/250px-8mm_Lebel_Tarbes-01.jpg)](/wiki/File:8mm_Lebel_Tarbes-01.jpg)

The [8mm Lebel](/wiki/8mm_Lebel "8mm Lebel") ammunition, developed in 1886, was the first [smokeless gunpowder](/wiki/Smokeless_powder "Smokeless powder") cartridge to be created and adopted by any country.

Most of the early all-metallic cartridges were of the [pinfire](/wiki/Pinfire "Pinfire") and [rimfire](/wiki/Rimfire_ammunition "Rimfire ammunition") types.

The first centerfire metallic cartridge was invented by [Jean Samuel Pauly](/wiki/Jean_Samuel_Pauly "Jean Samuel Pauly") in the first decades of the 19th century. However, although it was the first cartridge to use a form of [obturation](/wiki/Obturation "Obturation"), a feature integral to a successful breech-loading cartridge, Pauly died before it was converted to percussion cap ignition.

Frenchman [Louis-Nicolas Flobert](/wiki/Louis-Nicolas_Flobert "Louis-Nicolas Flobert") invented the first [rimfire metallic cartridge](/wiki/Rimfire_ammunition "Rimfire ammunition") in 1845. His cartridge consisted of a percussion cap with a bullet attached to the top.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) Flobert then made what he called "[parlor guns](/wiki/Gallery_gun "Gallery gun")" for this cartridge, as these rifles and pistols were designed to be shot in indoor shooting parlors in large homes.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-BarnesBodinson2009-39) These [6mm Flobert](/wiki/6mm_Flobert "6mm Flobert") cartridges do not contain any powder. The only [propellant](/wiki/Propellant "Propellant") substance contained in the cartridge is the percussion cap.[[40]](#cite_note-40) In English-speaking countries, the 6mm Flobert cartridge corresponds to [.22 BB Cap](/wiki/.22_BB_Cap ".22 BB Cap") and [.22 CB Cap](/wiki/.22_CB_Cap ".22 CB Cap") ammunition. These cartridges have a relatively low muzzle velocity of around 700 ft/s (210 m/s).

French gunsmith [Benjamin Houllier](/w/index.php?title=Benjamin_Houllier&action=edit&redlink=1 "Benjamin Houllier (page does not exist)") improved the Lefaucheux pinfire cardboard cartridge and patented in Paris in 1846, the first fully metallic pinfire cartridge containing powder in a metallic cartridge.[[34]](#cite_note-Pistols-34)[[41]](#cite_note-41) He also included in his patent claims rim and centerfire primed cartridges using brass or copper casings.[[31]](#cite_note-sil.si.edu-31) Houllier commercialised his weapons in association with the gunsmiths Blanchard or Charles Robert.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

In the United States, in 1857, the Flobert cartridge inspired the [.22 Short](/wiki/.22_Short ".22 Short"), specially conceived for the first American revolver using rimfire cartridges, the [Smith & Wesson Model 1](/wiki/Smith_%26_Wesson_Model_1 "Smith & Wesson Model 1"). A year before, in 1856, the [LeMat revolver](/wiki/LeMat_revolver "LeMat revolver") was the first American breech-loading firearm, but it used pinfire cartridges, not rimfire. Formerly, an employee of the [Colt's Patent Firearms Manufacturing Company](/wiki/Colt%27s_Manufacturing_Company "Colt's Manufacturing Company"), [Rollin White](/wiki/Rollin_White "Rollin White"), had been the first in America to conceive the idea of having the revolver cylinder bored through to accept metallic cartridges ([circa](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/circa "wikt:circa") 1852), with the first in the world to use bored-through cylinders probably having been Lefaucheux in 1845, who invented a pepperbox-revolver loaded from the rear using bored-through cylinders.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Another possible claimant for the bored-through cylinder is a Frenchman by the name of Perrin, who allegedly produced in 1839 a pepperbox revolver with a bored-through cylinder to order. Other possible claimants include Devisme of France in 1834 or 1842 who claimed to have produced a breech-loading revolver in that period though his claim was later judged as lacking in evidence by French courts and Hertog & Devos and Malherbe & Rissack of Belgium who both filed patents for breech-loading revolvers in 1853.[[45]](#cite_note-45) However, [Samuel Colt](/wiki/Samuel_Colt "Samuel Colt") refused this innovation. White left Colt, went to Smith & Wesson to rent a license for his patent, and this is how the S&W Model 1 saw the light of day in 1857. The patent didn't definitively expire until 1870, allowing Smith & Wesson competitors to design and commercialize their own revolving breech-loaders using metallic cartridges. Famous models of that time are the Colt [Open Top](/wiki/Colt_Model_1871-72_Open_Top "Colt Model 1871-72 Open Top") (1871–1872) and [Single Action Army "Peacemaker"](/wiki/Colt_Single_Action_Army "Colt Single Action Army") (1873). But in rifles, the [lever-action](/wiki/Lever-action "Lever-action") mechanism patents were not obstructed by Rollin White's [patent infringement](/wiki/Patent_infringement "Patent infringement") because White only held a patent concerning drilled cylinders and revolving mechanisms. Thus, larger caliber rimfire cartridges were soon introduced after 1857, when the Smith & Wesson .22 Short ammunition was introduced for the first time. Some of these rifle cartridges were used in the American Civil War, including the [.44 Henry](/wiki/.44_Henry ".44 Henry") and [56-56 Spencer](/wiki/56-56_Spencer "56-56 Spencer") (both in 1860). However, the large rimfire cartridges were soon replaced by [centerfire](/wiki/Centerfire "Centerfire") cartridges, which could safely handle higher pressures.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-Williamson-47)

In 1867, the British war office adopted the [Eley](/wiki/Eley_Brothers "Eley Brothers")–[Boxer](/wiki/Edward_Mounier_Boxer "Edward Mounier Boxer") metallic centerfire cartridge case in the [Pattern 1853 Enfield](/wiki/Pattern_1853_Enfield "Pattern 1853 Enfield") rifles, which were converted to [Snider-Enfield](/wiki/Snider-Enfield "Snider-Enfield") breech-loaders on the Snider principle. This consisted of a block opening on a hinge, thus forming a false breech against which the cartridge rested. The priming cap was in the base of the cartridge and was discharged by a [striker](/wiki/Firing_pin#Firing_pin_vs._striker "Firing pin") passing through the breech block. Other European powers adopted breech-loading military rifles from 1866 to 1868, with paper instead of metallic cartridge cases. The original Eley-Boxer cartridge case was made of thin-coiled brass—occasionally these cartridges could break apart and jam the breech with the unwound remains of the case upon firing. Later the solid-drawn, centerfire cartridge case, made of one entire solid piece of tough hard metal, an alloy of copper, with a solid head of thicker metal, has been generally substituted.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Centerfire cartridges with solid-drawn metallic cases containing their own means of ignition are almost universally used in all modern varieties of military and sporting rifles and pistols.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Around 1870, machined tolerances had improved to the point that the cartridge case was no longer necessary to seal a firing chamber. Precision-faced bolts would seal as well, and could be economically manufactured.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, normal wear and tear proved this system to be generally infeasible.

## Nomenclature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cartridge_(firearms)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Nomenclature")]

The name of any given cartridge does not necessarily reflect any cartridge or gun dimension. The name is merely the standardized and accepted moniker. SAAMI (Sporting Arms and Ammunition Manufacturers' Institute) and the European counterpart (CIP) and members of those organizations specify correct cartridge names.

It is incomplete to refer to a cartridge as a certain "[caliber](/wiki/Caliber "Caliber")" (e.g., "30-06 caliber"), as the word *caliber* only describes the bullet diameter. The correct full name for this round is [.30–'06 Springfield](/wiki/.30-06_Springfield ".30-06 Springfield"). The "-'06" means it was introduced in 1906. In sporting arms, the only consistent definition of "caliber" is bore diameter, and dozens of unique [.30-caliber](/wiki/.30-caliber ".30-caliber") round types exist.

There is considerable variation in cartridge nomenclature. Names sometimes reflect various characteristics of the cartridge. For example, the .308 Winchester uses a bullet of 308/1000-inch diameter and was standardized by Winchester. Conversely, cartridge names often reflect nothing related to the cartridge in any obvious way. For example, the .218 Bee uses a bullet of 224/1000-inch diameter, fired through a .22-in bore, etc. The 218 and Bee portions of this cartridge name reflect nothing other than the desires of those who standardized that cartridge. Many similar examples exist, for example: .219 Zipper, .221 Fireball, .222 Remington, .256 Winchester, .280 Remington, .307 Winchester, .356 Winchester.

Where two numbers are used in a cartridge name, the second number may reflect a variety of things. Frequently the first number reflects bore diameter (inches or millimeters). The second number reflects case length (in inches or mm). For example, the [7.62×51mm NATO](/wiki/7.62%C3%9751mm_NATO "7.62×51mm NATO") refers to a bore diameter of 7.62 mm and an overall case length of 51 mm, with a total length of 71.1 mm. The commercial version is the [.308 Winchester](/wiki/.308_Winchester ".308 Winchester").

In older [black powder](/wiki/Black_powder "Black powder") cartridges, the second number typically refers to powder charge, in [grains](/wiki/Grain_(unit) "Grain (unit)"). For example, the [.50-90 Sharps](/wiki/.50-90_Sharps ".50-90 Sharps") has a .50-inch bore and used a nominal charge of 90.0 grains (5.83 g) of black powder.

Many such cartridges were designated by a three-number system (e.g., 45–120–31⁄4 Sharps: 45-caliber bore, 120 grains of (black) powder, 31⁄4-inch long case). Other times, a similar three-number system indicated bore (caliber), charge (grains), and bullet weight (grains). The 45-70-500 Government is an example.

Often, the name reflects the company or individual who standardized it, such as the [.30 Newton](/wiki/.30_Newton ".30 Newton"), or some characteristic important to that person.

The [.38 Special](/wiki/.38_Special ".38 Special") actually has a nominal bullet diameter of 0.3570 inches (9.07 mm) (jacketed) or 0.3580 inches (9.09 mm) (lead) while the case has a nominal diameter of 0.3800 inches (9.65 mm), hence the name. This is historically logical: the hole drilled through the chambers of .36-caliber cap-and-ball revolvers when converting those to work with cartridges was 0.3800 inches (9.65 mm), and the cartridge made to work in those revolvers was logically named the [.38 Colt](/wiki/.38_Short_Colt ".38 Short Colt"). The original cartridges used a heeled bullet like a .22 rimfire where the bullet was the same diameter as the case. Early Colt Army .38s have a bore diameter that will allow a .357" diameter bullet to slide through the barrel. The cylinder is bored straight through with no step. Later versions used an inside the case lubricated bullet of .357" diameter instead of the original .38" with a reduction in bore diameter. The difference in .38 Special bullet diameter and case diameter reflects the thickness of the case mouth (approximately 11/1000-inch per side). The [.357 Magnum](/wiki/.357_Magnum ".357 Magnum") evolved from the .38 Special. The .357 was named to reflect bullet diameter (in thousandths inch), not case diameter. "Magnum" was used to indicate its longer case and higher operating pressure.

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cartridge_(firearms)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Classification")]

Cartridges are classified by some major characteristics. One classification is the location of the primer. Early cartridges began with the pinfire, then the rimfire, and finally the centerfire.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Simpified_schematic_of_blowback_mechanism_with_advanced_primer_ignition.png/250px-Simpified_schematic_of_blowback_mechanism_with_advanced_primer_ignition.png)](/wiki/File:Simpified_schematic_of_blowback_mechanism_with_advanced_primer_ignition.png)

Schematic of an Advanced Primer Blowback operation using a rebated rim cartridge (bottlenecked). The rebated rim allows the bolt to enter the chamber with the round to be fired in forward travel just before the cartridge in-battery position.

Another classification describes how cartridges are located in the chamber ([headspace](/wiki/Headspace_(firearms) "Headspace (firearms)")). Rimmed cartridges are located with the rim near the cartridge head; the rim is also used to extract the cartridge from the chamber. Examples are the [.22 long rifle](/wiki/.22_long_rifle ".22 long rifle") and [.303 British](/wiki/.303_British ".303 British"). In a rimless cartridge, the cartridge head diameter is about the same as or smaller than the body diameter. The head will have a groove so the cartridge can be extracted from the chamber. Locating the cartridge in the chamber is accomplished by other means. Some rimless cartridges are necked down, and they are positioned by the cartridge's shoulder. An example is the [.30-06 Springfield](/wiki/.30-06_Springfield ".30-06 Springfield"). Pistol cartridges may be located by the end of the brass case. An example is the [.45 ACP](/wiki/.45_ACP ".45 ACP"). A [belted cartridge](/wiki/Belted_magnum "Belted magnum") has a larger diameter band of thick metal near the head of the cartridge. An example is the [.300 Weatherby Magnum](/wiki/.300_Weatherby_Magnum ".300 Weatherby Magnum"). An extreme version of the rimless cartridge is the rebated case; guns employing [advanced primer ignition](/wiki/Blowback_(firearms)#Advanced_primer_ignition_(API)_blowback "Blowback (firearms)") need such a case because the case moves during firing (i.e., it is not located at a fixed position). An example is the [20mm×110RB](/wiki/Oerlikon_20_mm_cannon "Oerlikon 20 mm cannon").

### Centerfire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cartridge_(firearms)&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Centerfire")]

Main article: [Centerfire ammunition](/wiki/Centerfire_ammunition "Centerfire ammunition")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Fired_rimfire_and_centerfire_casings.jpg/250px-Fired_rimfire_and_centerfire_casings.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fired_rimfire_and_centerfire_casings.jpg)

Fired rimfire (left) and centerfire (right) cartridges. A rimfire [firing pin](/wiki/Firing_pin "Firing pin") produces a notch at the edge of the rim; a centerfire pin produces a divot in the center of the primer.

A centerfire cartridge has a centrally located primer held within a recess in the case head. Most centerfire brass cases used worldwide for sporting ammunition use [Boxer primers](/wiki/Boxer_primer "Boxer primer"). It is easy to remove and replace Boxer primers using standard reloading tools, facilitating reuse.

Some European- and Asian-manufactured military and sporting ammunition uses [Berdan primers](/wiki/Berdan_primer "Berdan primer"). Removing the spent primer from (decapping) these cases requires the use of a special tool because the primer anvil (on which the primer compound is crushed) is an integral part of the case and the case, therefore, does not have a central hole through which a decapping tool can push the primer out from the inside, as is done with Boxer primers. In Berdan cases, the flash holes are located to the sides of the anvil. With the right tool and components, reloading Berdan-primed cases is perfectly feasible. However, Berdan primers are not readily available in the U.S.

### Rimfire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cartridge_(firearms)&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Rimfire")]

Main article: [Rimfire ammunition](/wiki/Rimfire_ammunition "Rimfire ammunition")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Rim_fire.svg/250px-Rim_fire.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Rim_fire.svg)

Schematic of a rimfire cartridge and its ignition

Rimfire priming was a popular solution before centerfire priming was perfected. In a rimfire case, centrifugal force pushes a liquid priming compound into the internal recess of the folded rim as the manufacturer spins the case at a high rate and heats the spinning case to dry the priming compound mixture in place within the hollow cavity formed within the rim fold at the perimeter of the case interior.

In the mid to late 19th century, many rimfire cartridge designs existed. Today only a few, mostly for use in small-caliber guns, remain in general and widespread use. These include the .17 Mach II, .17 Hornady Magnum Rimfire (HMR), 5mm Remington Magnum (Rem Mag), .22 (BB, CB, Short, Long, Long Rifle), and .22 Winchester Magnum Rimfire (WMR).

Compared to modern centerfire cases used in the strongest types of modern guns, existing rimfire cartridge designs use loads that generate relatively low chamber pressures because of limitations of feasible gun design, as the rim has little or no lateral support from the gun. Such support would require very close tolerances in the design of the chamber, bolt, and firing pin. Because that is not cost-effective method, it is necessary to keep rimfire load pressure low enough so that the stress generated by chamber pressure would not push the case rim outward and cause the rim to expand significantly. Also, the wall of the folded rim must be both thin and ductile enough to easily deform, as necessary to allow the blow from the firing pin to crush the rim, thereby igniting the primer compound, and it must do so without rupturing the case. If the rim is too thick, it will be too resistant to deformation and if it is too hard, the rim will be to brittle and crack, rather than deform.[[47]](#cite_note-Williamson-47)

Modern centerfire cartridges are often loaded to 65,000 psi (450 MPa) maximum chamber pressure. Conversely, no commercialized rimfire has ever been loaded above 40,000 psi (280 MPa) maximum chamber pressure. However, with careful gun design and production, no fundamental reason exists that higher pressures could not be used. Despite the relative lower chamber pressure, modern rimfire magnums are commonly found in .17 caliber (4.5 mm), .20 caliber (5 mm), and .22 caliber (5.6 mm) that can generate muzzle energies comparable to smaller caliber centerfire cartridges.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Today, [.22 LR](/wiki/.22_LR ".22 LR") (.22 Long Rifle) accounts for the vast majority of all rimfire ammunition produced. Standard .22 LR rounds use an essentially pure lead bullet plated with a typical 95% copper, 5% zinc combination. These are offered in [supersonic](/wiki/Supersonic "Supersonic") and subsonic types, as well as target shooting, plinking, and hunting versions. These cartridges are usually coated with hard wax for fouling control.

The .22 LR and related .22 rimfire cartridges use a [heeled bullet](/wiki/Heeled_bullet "Heeled bullet"), where the external diameter of the case is the same as the diameter of the forward portion of the bullet and where the rearward portion of the bullet, which extends into the case, is necessarily smaller in diameter than the main body of the bullet.

## Semi-automatic vs. revolver cartridges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cartridge_(firearms)&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Semi-automatic vs. revolver cartridges")]

Most [revolver](/wiki/Revolver "Revolver") cartridges are rimmed at the base of the case, which seats against the edge of the [cylinder](/wiki/Cylinder_(firearms) "Cylinder (firearms)") [chamber](/wiki/Chamber_(firearms) "Chamber (firearms)") to provide [headspace](/wiki/Headspace_(firearms) "Headspace (firearms)") control (to keep the cartridge from moving too far forward into the chamber) and to facilitate easy extraction.

Nearly every [centerfire](/wiki/Centerfire "Centerfire") [semi-automatic pistol](/wiki/Semi-automatic_pistol "Semi-automatic pistol") cartridge is "rimless", where the rim is of the same diameter as the case body but separated by a circumferential groove in between, into which the [extractor](/wiki/Extractor_(firearms) "Extractor (firearms)") engages the rim by hooking. A "semi-rimmed" cartridge is essentially a rimless one but the rim diameter is slightly larger than the case body, and a "rebated rimless" cartridge is one with the rim smaller in diameter. All such cartridges' headspace on the case mouth (although some, such as [.38 Super](/wiki/.38_Super ".38 Super"), at one time seated on the rim, this was changed for accuracy reasons), which prevents the round from entering too far into the chamber. Some cartridges have a rim that is significantly smaller than the case body diameter. These are known as rebated-rim designs and almost always allow a handgun to fire multiple caliber cartridges with only a barrel and [magazine](/wiki/Magazine_(firearms) "Magazine (firearms)") change.
